segments.text: Stay tuned now for Edward R. Murrow with the news presented by Hams, the beer refreshing.
Hams is the beer that looks refreshing, dancing gold in your glass, tastes refreshing, crisp and clean cut with smoothness aged in.
Yes, Hams is the beer that captures for you the refreshing enchantment of the land it comes from, the land of sky blue waters.
When you're dry and thirsty, ask for hands, H, A, Double M, S, Hands, refreshingly yours
from the land of sky blue waters. Edward R. Murrow is now in Europe for an
on-the-spot survey of the leading capitals and interviews with the men and women
who make the news. In a few minutes we will hear Mr. Murrow's report, but first
years Bill Shadell with the summary of the news.
Good evening.
The big news tonight is, of course, in Britain, where returns are now coming in on the British
national elections.
Edward R. Murrow is standing by in London to bring you his report on this day's voting.
We take you now direct to London and Edward R. Murrow.
That is London.
The polls closed and Britain's national election about four hours ago.
Here is the way the party stand at the moment.
We have 100 results out of 625.
The labor party have 156.
The conservatives, 43 and the liberals won.
The refrain all evening as the reports have come in from the constituencies has been no change, no change.
So far the labor party has lost five seats in parliament,
four of them going to the conservative and one to the liberals.
In terms of the total popular vote, there has been a swing of about 1.5% to the conservative.
If this trend continues, it will probably mean a narrow majority of perhaps 20 for the conservatives in the new House of Commons.
These early returns, of course, are in no sense conclusive.
But it may be possible to draw certain conclusions from them.
There is probably no man in Britain who has spent more time analyzing the past,
present on future status of British political opinion.
Then Dr. Henry Doran, the director of the Gellipoll,
is predictions in the past have had a consistent record of accuracy.
Dr. Doran, what's happening to the liberal vote in this election?
The present indication is that although the liberal can't see church as seat
and contested only just over 100 instead of nearly 500 as they did last time.
In spite of that careful selection, it seems that their vote is goffing in those 100 constituents.
Well, is it going in the constituencies where there is no liberal candidate?
Is it going more to the conservatives or more to labor?
Uh, I estimate that it's going to the conservatives about two to one.
Well, now before the election, you predicted that the conservatives would come in with a small majority.
Have you seen anything in the figures tonight
to cause you to change that opinion?
At the moment, it looks as though that will be the case.
You still feel that it will be perhaps
the majority of 20 for the conservatives.
I guess so at the present time yet.
Now, Dr. Durant, what's been happening
to the candidates who have been supported by an Iron Bevan,
the left-wing leader of the Labour Party?
They seem to have done at least as well
as the ordinary Labour candidates,
and in fact, there is some indication
that they have done better.
there were two seats in the same count where one labor and both held by labor
man one was a bevernite and with the other one wasn't the bevernite has
retained his seat the other man the official labor man has locked his seat now
you're drawing a conclusion so far about the communist vote and there hasn't
been any evidence at all because they are contesting only ten seats and so
far there have been no return concerning their seats what's happened to the
some total of the popular vote. I believe it was 84% in the last election. What does it look like
this time? It seems it's going to be very close to 84 again. On the basis of the return so far,
then, in your opinion, your original estimate still stands. I think so, yes. Yes. Do you feel that
in the course of the night, when they return to begin to come in from the north, that there is likely
to be any substantial change in this trend that has been established now? I doubt it. The trend
So far, as we can see, it was universal.
And it's your opinion that that will continue to after.
Is it dangerous to have such a guest,
but if I have to, I would say yes.
Well, thank you very much indeed, Dr. Durant.
I've never seen an election quite like this one.
It seems to me that the only neutrals in London today
were the pigeons in Travouder Square
and the leaves that were burning in the park here in London.
The crowds that were entering the polling booths from lands and to John Egrotes were quiet and orderly.
The British take their voting like they take their pleasure, very seriously.
Here in London, there was a grim seriousness to be seen on the faces of those who went in to mark their ballot.
Most of them looked as old they would like to vote three or four times if the law allowed it.
They stood patiently in line waiting their turn.
They are accustomed to standing in line.
They stand in line for their food, for their butters,
and for their paychecks.
But there was very little light-hearted banter
in this election here in London today.
It was a serious business.
There was what I would call a grim note about the whole thing.
Much more than last year or in the election of 1945.
This has been a fairly well-mannered election on the surface,
but the real bitterness underlying the whole thing
All things can't be concealed.
And the result, whatever it is,
will leave a great deal of hatred.
And much of that hatred will be based upon class.
The weather has been good throughout the island.
And the building has been heavy.
As Dr. Durant indicated a moment ago,
it may well go above the 84% registered in the last election.
Here in London tonight,
one of the favorite power games
is getting the composition of the new conservative cabinet.
But the feeling still persists that anything can happen,
although Dr. Durant, who has been right more often
than most of us, still feels that the conservatives
will come in with a majority of 20.
I think it would be fair to say that there
was almost a sense of quiet desperation
about the voting here in London today.
Watching the crowds, I had the feeling
that their minds had been made up.
But it hadn't been easy, what did their conviction with firm?
There are a lot of parties going on here in London tonight,
one for a couple of thousand notables at the Savoy Hotel,
given by a conservative publisher.
At last report, the conservatives in attendance
were not jubilant as a result.
But it's much too early for gloom or jubilation.
And everyone realizes, of course,
that if the conservatives come in with a majority of only 15
They will lead a very precarious existence as the government of the United Kingdom.
Mr. Churchill and a number of his shadow cabinets are listening to the results that is home in Hyde Park Gate.
And many places in London, people who have saved the Baja have broached it.
The toast to the future of their party, uncertain as that future may be at the moment.
Throughout this campaign, it has appeared that the two parties were grinding against each other on old issues
Without damaging each other substantially and the results reported so far do not indicate that either property has drowned very much off the other
The latest report just handed me show labor with 71 feet the conservatives with 54
The Liberals with one that means that labor has lost five feet that the conservatives have gained four and the Liberals have gained one
If you did, Morrow and London, I return you now to Bill Shaddell and New York.
The Allies has spelled out their proposals for a ceasefire line in Korea based roughly
on present battle lines.
The Reds counter proposals may come tonight in the second session of the New Armistice
talk scheduled within the hour.
Meanwhile, full-scale air war appears to be shaping up over Korea with the Reds for
the fifth straight day, making determined efforts to break up the Allied raids, which were
concentrated today on communist supply lines.
Our Air Force claims nine locomotives destroyed, 19 others damaged, and one red-plained damage.
The Reds again sent up more than 80 jet fighters to challenge our fighter bombers.
In Washington, the critical talks with Iranian Premier Mosadek, over the British Iranian
oil dispute, shifted today from the Capitol to a far-mouted nearby Middleburg, Virginia,
As Assistant Secretary of State George McGee entertained the visiting premier,
McGee took over the discussions since Secretary Atchison and the Party of Delegates to the
UN Assembly were leaving today for Paris.
President Truman says he feels some progress is being made in the discussions.
Iran signed a trade agreement with Russia today, giving Russia Iranian cotton an exchange
for sugar now denied Iran in the British retaliation over oil cancellations.
papers made much today over diplomatic talks between Egypt and Russia, overshadowing the
American ambassador's talks with King Faroo.
In Washington, the State Department denies any U.S. move to mediate the bitter dispute
between Egypt and Britain.
A British military spokesman admits the boycott by Egyptian labor is creating a critical
situation for the British in the Canal Zone.
France's Foreign Minister Shuman promised today that if Italy's request for UN membership
is vetoed in the Security Council, the question will be taken up in the General Assembly, where
a veto is ineffective. President Truman admitted to Newsman today that the controversy over
his nomination of General Mark Clark as ambassador to the Vatican, that was not as great as he
had expected. A strong redefended his action said there was still plenty of time for the criticism
to be aired before Congress takes up the nomination in January.
The Agriculture Department tonight predicts higher prices in dairy products.
Higher prices for clothing, rents, and food sent the government's cost of living index
today to a new record high, which means another round of wage increases for thousands of
workers covered by cost of living contracts.
Senator Taft has called for a recheck of military expenditures by a joint civilian military
commission, since we've just about reached the limit, he says, on taxes. The Senate Preparedness
Committee issued another report today criticizing waste of manpower at military bases of the Navy and
Marine Corps. A collector of taxes in Brooklyn who was asked to resign this week told House
investigators today he perhaps tried last February to get an investigation of his office
postpone. A federal grand jury, again indicted, former Commerce Department employee William
Remington, on five counts of perjury. Remington had just won an appeal to have a former perjury
conviction reversed. While 12 million people here in New York and in surrounding areas were
cut off from their normal delivery of milk today in a strike of AFL Teamsters, which is now settled.
Striking dockworkers throughout the port of New York agreed earlier today to move some cargoes
of military peers, then apparently change their minds as the strike is extended to other eastern
ports. This is Bill Siddell. Ed Murrow will be back in a moment with the word for today.
These are the days when a scent of burning leaves fills the air. Everywhere folks are busily
ricking their yards, planting tulip bulbs, cutting fireplace wood, and getting ready for old man
winter. And when it comes time for a break, that's when Ham's beer appears on the scene.
For every day, all over America, more and more people are claiming Ham's beer as their
kind of beer for real refreshment. Ham's alone, among the country's finest beers, captures
an exhilarating bit of the refreshing land where it's brewed, the land of sky blue waters.
No matter where you are, when real refreshments call for, that extra refreshing Northland zest
is yours to enjoy.
You see it in the golden autumn color of hams.
You enjoy it in the crisp, clean cut taste with smoothness aged in.
Whenever you want something really special in the way of refreshment these busy fall days,
just help yourself.
a frosty cold, ham's beer, refreshingly yours from the land of sky blue waters.
And now, Ed, what's the word for today?
Give them a second.
Instead of the word for today, I will give you the latest standing of the parties.
Labor has 181 seats, the Conservatives 68 and the Liberals 1.
And I think perhaps there is time and up, um, Bob Vickson to say that, uh, we may have
hoped that Mr. Will Rogers would write when he said, a politician is not as narrow-minded
as he forces himself to be.
Good night, and good luck.
Tune in again tomorrow night for Edward R. Murrow and the News, brought to you by the
Ham Brewing Company of St. Paul, Minnesota.
Tonight, reach for a bottle of Ham's beer, the beer with the crisp, clean cut flavor all
its own.
It's truly America's most refreshing beer, from the land of sky blue waters in Chatted
Land of Cool Refreshment.
Ask for Ham's beer tonight.
Listen tomorrow, tomorrow.
He Flynn speaking.
This is the CDS radio network.
